turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: To call in Spanish, so to call to somebody and also to make a phone call is llamar.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: L-l, and we said this double l creates a / yuh / sound in most Spanish dialects just like the y. L-l-a- m-a-r. Llamar.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. And we said that in Spanish you don’t tend to say my name is or I am called, you say I call myself to introduce yourself. So how would you say I call myself?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Me llamo. Good. And if you want to ask somebody what their name is, you could say how do you call yourself? So let’s do this informally. How do you call yourself? What was the word for how?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Cómo. How do you call yourself?:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. ¿ Cómo te llamas? How would you say how does she call herself? What is her name?:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. ¿ Cómo se llama? Imagine you heard her name once before and you wanted to say what was she called? How was she calling herself?:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. ¿ Cómo se llamaba? What was her name, what was his name, or what was your name (formally)? What was their name, what were they calling themselves?:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. ¿ Cómo se llamaban? Good. What was to have, not in the sense of haber, not the have that we use to go into the past, but the have that we used for possession?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Obtener. Maintain changed a little bit. Have a go:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Mantener.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Mantener. And we said that man- actually is like from mano, hand. Sustain. And this one changed a little bit as well.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Sostener. So this - tain that we have in English is tener. To have. So how would you say I used to have?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Tenía. Good. So if you wanted to say I used to have a big house:

turns.speaker: student
expected_answers: Tenía un grande casa Un casa grande

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Yeah, almost, but un?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. These bits and pieces require a little thought as well. Tenía una casa grande. We used to have a big house:

turns.speaker: student
expected_answers: Teníamos una grande - una casa grande

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Teníamos una casa grande. And the accent stays in the same place on the - ía. Tenía, teníamos. Good. I used to have four, but now I don’t have any left. I used to have four, but now I don’t have any left: the word for four is cuatro, cuatro.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Related to:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Very good. So I used to have four:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. But now:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: So, or I don’t have any left: say to give that

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. So all together.:

turns.speaker: student
expected_answers: Tenía cuatro, pero ahora no me queda

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Tenía cuatro, pero ahora no me queda or pero ahora, no me quedan. You could use either. So, we are not using have to give this meaning we have left. How was I have in the present?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Tengo. And how did you make that I have to? I have to in the sense of I must:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: So I have to see you

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Tengo que verte. Now if you want to say I had to see you, you can use this line in the past that we learned even though in English we are saying had, not having to, this is fine in Spanish. So I had to see you, I was having to see you:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Tenía que verte. Good. Yesterday is ayer, a-y-e-r. Ayer.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. So I had to see you yesterday:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Tenía que verte ayer. To do or to make was hacer. H-a-c-e-r. Hacer.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Did you have to do it yesterday? Talking informally, did you have to do it yesterday?:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. ¿ Tenías que hacerlo ayer?:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. And you could use this like ¿ No tenías que hacerlo ayer? Didn’t you have to do it yesterday? Weren’t you supposed to do it yesterday? Good. I want to look at just three irregular verbs that we have for this line in the past. Now this is a very regular tense. There are very few irregularities in this tense. It’s a very safe tense and the irregularities that we have are with very small verbs. For example, ver. How was I see, from ver?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Veo. The same irregularity that we get in the I form in the present, veo; we get it in this line in the past. So instead of taking off - er and putting - o, we ended up with this extra e. So veía is I was seeing, I used to see, he was seeing, he used to see, she was seeing, she used to see, or you (formal). Veía. Give me the other versions then. We were seeing:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Veíamos. They or you guys:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Veían. You (informal):

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Another irregular verb in this tense is ser. Ser. To be. Now the base for I, he, she, it, you (formal) is era, era. Now this is very easy to remember because we are looking at the past which is a line in time and then era in English is a line in time in the past. So we can just think of an era. We would use this with characteristic things. So for example, if you wanted to say I used to be a student, what was student?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. So how would you say I used to be a student?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Era estudiante. Good. We don’t need the word for a. Era estudiante. Good. Were you a student, informally?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. ¿ Eras estudiante? We were students.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Where are you putting the accent? I couldn’t tell., but it’s

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Éramos era, éramos. Good. We were students:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. What were they? What were they?:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Yeah, ¿Qué eran? Good. And then the last one of course, that is irregular in this tense is ir. Ir. To go. Now I was going is kind of like a blend of the two different ways we have of creating this past: - aba and -ía. I was going is iba. So you see we get a bit of -ía and a bit of - aba with ir. I was going from to go. Iba. So you can give me the other versions. We were going:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. They were going:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. You were going, informally:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Ibas. Good. Why were you going?:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Now iba is very useful because, of course, you can use it like in the English I was going to call you, for example. I was going to go, but I didn’t have the time. I was going to do it, but I forgot. So you can use it in this way as well, which is very common. So how would you say I was going to call you, and we are talking informally? I was going to call you:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: There is something missing. Iba llamarte.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Hmm, what kind of verb is going?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Iba a llamarte. I was going to do it:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. And I’m not sure here if you are putting the a to connect the verbs.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Ok, so again.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Iba a hacerlo. So actually in fluent speech you would hear / ibacerlo /. All of those three / a /’s, iba, a, hacer would blend together, so that’s why I wasn’t sure if you said it or not. We were going to do it or make it:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Íbamos a hacerlo. We were going to do them or we were going to make them:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Íbamos a hacerlos. Good.
